era_context: The Akkadian Empire under Naram-Sin is consolidating power and expanding militarily. Egypt's Old Kingdom (Dynasty VI) continues its administrative traditions. The Indus Valley Civilization is in its mature urban phase. This is a period of high centralization and monumental achievement in the Old World's major civilizations, though underlying instabilities are present.

events.title: Naram-Sin's campaigns in the Zagros and Anatolia
events.description: Naram-Sin continues military campaigns to consolidate Akkadian control over the Zagros Mountains and parts of Anatolia. These campaigns are directed against the Lullubi, Gutians, and other highland peoples who resist Akkadian domination. The campaigns are commemorated in royal inscriptions and the Victory Stele of Naram-Sin. These military efforts are resource-intensive and contribute to the strain on the Akkadian economy.

sources.name: Akkadian royal inscriptions and the Victory Stele

events.certainty_note: Naram-Sin's military campaigns are well-attested in contemporary inscriptions. The specific year 2367 BCE is assigned within his reign by convention.

events.title: Egyptian Dynasty VI administrative activity
events.description: Egypt under Dynasty VI continues its administrative and religious institutions. The pharaoh maintains control over the priesthood, military, and bureaucracy, though provincial governors are gaining increasing autonomy. Pyramid construction continues, though at reduced scale. Trade with Nubia and the Levant remains active. The period is characterized by stability but also by the gradual decentralization that will lead to the First Intermediate Period.
key_figures: Pharaoh of Dynasty VI (specific ruler uncertain)

sources.name: Egyptian administrative inscriptions and tomb records

events.certainty_note: Dynasty VI is dated c. 2278–2184 BCE; the specific events of 2367 BCE are not individually documented.

disconfirming_evidence: No specific disconfirming evidence for 2367 BCE. However, the extent of Akkadian territorial control remains debated among scholars; some argue that Naram-Sin's inscriptions represent ideological claims rather than actual administrative control.
historiographic_note: The year 2367 BCE is not individually attested in any surviving source. The events described are inferred from the broader context of Naram-Sin's reign and Dynasty VI Egypt. The historical record is sparse and heavily weighted toward elite political and military activities.

graph_edges.note: Part of Naram-Sin's broader military consolidation.
